title: About
text:
This site is a personal data journalism project focused on college and
      professional basketball. Every post is built from raw game-by-game data —
      no box-score summaries, no recycled narratives. Just the data, visualized
      and interpreted.
    
    
      A basketball fan who believes the best stories in sports are hiding in the
      data. Built this site to share those stories publicly.

title: About
text:
The Stack







Tool
Purpose




R
Data ingestion, wrangling, and visualization (tidyverse, ggplot2)


Quarto
Rendering and publishing


GitHub Pages
Hosting


ESPN API
Game-by-game data source





Questions, feedback, or data requests? Reach out on GitHub.

title: Classroom to Court
text:
Basketball has always been more than a game — it’s a laboratory of strategy, athleticism, and storytelling. Classroom to Court bridges the gap between the academic study of sport and the hardwood itself, turning raw statistics into narratives that every fan can appreciate.
Whether you follow the high-stakes drama of March Madness or track every NBA standings shift across an 82-game season, this site gives you something most coverage doesn’t: the numbers behind the moments. Every chart, every breakdown, and every post here is built from real game-by-game data — no filler, no recycled takes, just basketball and the statistics that explain it.

title: New York Yankees
text:
The New York Yankees’ 2024 season ran all the way to the World Series before ending in a loss to the Dodgers. Below is a game-by-game breakdown of their full postseason run.






Postseason Record: 8-6 | Runs Scored: 67 | Runs Allowed: 57 | Run Differential: +10 | Extra-Inning Games: 3




Beat the KCR 3-1 in the ALDS, beat the CLE 4-1 in the ALCS, then fell to the LAD 1-4 in the World Series.

title: New York Yankees
section: Runs Scored vs. Runs Allowed by Game
text:
Runs Scored vs. Runs Allowed by Game


Show Code
team |>
  select(game_label, R, RA) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(R, RA), names_to = "type", values_to = "runs") |>
  mutate(type = recode(type, R = "Runs Scored", RA = "Runs Allowed")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = game_label, y = runs, fill = type)) +
  geom_col(position = "dodge", width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("Runs Scored" = win_color, "Runs Allowed" = loss_color)) +
  scale_y_continuous(expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Runs Scored vs. Runs Allowed",
    subtitle = "New York Yankees | 2024 Postseason",
    x = NULL, y = "Runs", fill = NULL,
    caption = "Games in chronological order, grouped by round"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = team_primary),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 55, hjust = 1, size = 8),
        legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())










Insight: The Yankees’ offense disappeared in the World Series, averaging just 4.8 runs per game against the Dodgers versus 4.8 runs per game in the ALDS and ALCS combined.

title: New York Yankees
text:
Cumulative Win/Loss Record


Show Code
team |>
  select(game_label, cum_wins, cum_losses) |>
  pivot_longer(cols = c(cum_wins, cum_losses), names_to = "type", values_to = "count") |>
  mutate(type = recode(type, cum_wins = "Wins", cum_losses = "Losses")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = game_label, y = count, color = type, group = type)) +
  geom_line(linewidth = 1.1) +
  geom_point(size = 2.5) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Wins" = win_color, "Losses" = loss_color)) +
  labs(
    title    = "Cumulative Postseason Record",
    subtitle = "New York Yankees | 2024 Postseason",
    x = NULL, y = "Games", color = NULL
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = team_primary),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 55, hjust = 1, size = 8),
        legend.position = "top",
        panel.grid.major.x = element_blank(), panel.grid.minor = element_blank())

title: New York Yankees
text:
Attendance by Game


Show Code
ggplot(team, aes(x = game_label, y = attendance, fill = stage)) +
  geom_col(width = 0.7) +
  scale_fill_manual(values = c("ALDS" = team_secondary, "ALCS" = team_primary, "WS" = loss_color)) +
  scale_y_continuous(labels = comma, expand = expansion(mult = c(0, 0.08))) +
  labs(
    title    = "Attendance by Game",
    subtitle = "New York Yankees | 2024 Postseason",
    x = NULL, y = "Attendance", fill = "Round"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 12) +
  theme(plot.title = element_text(face = "bold", color = team_primary),
        plot.subtitle = element_text(color = "gray40"),
        axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 55, hjust = 1, size = 8),

title: New York Yankees
text:
Key Stats Summary


Show Code
summary_tbl <- tibble(
  Statistic = c("Games Played", "Record", "Runs Scored", "Runs Allowed",
                "Run Differential", "Runs Scored/Game", "Runs Allowed/Game",
                "Extra-Inning Games", "Average Attendance", "Highest Attendance"),
  Value = c(
    as.character(nrow(team)),
    paste0(overall_wins, "-", overall_losses),
    as.character(runs_scored),
    as.character(runs_allowed),
    sprintf("%+d", runs_scored - runs_allowed),
    sprintf("%.2f", mean(team$R)),
    sprintf("%.2f", mean(team$RA)),
    as.character(extra_games),
    comma(round(mean(team$attendance))),
    comma(max(team$attendance))
  )
)

knitr::kable(summary_tbl, align = c("l", "r"), col.names = c("Statistic", "Value"))

title: New York Yankees
text:
Key Takeaways
The Yankees’ 2024 postseason run was carried by dominant series wins over the Royals (3-1) and Guardians (4-1), outscoring both opponents across ten games. That offense went cold in the World Series against the Dodgers, where the Yankees managed just one win in five games and were outscored 25-24. 3 of the fourteen postseason games went to extra innings, underscoring how close the margins were in October.

title: Boston celtics linear regression
text:
library(corrgram)
library(corrplot)
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(gtExtras)

nba_team_Celtics_2026 <- read_excel("../../pipeline/nba/data/team_stats/nba_team_Celtics_2026.xlsx")

cor.data <- cor(nba_team_Celtics_2026[,num.cols])

library(corrplot)

numeric_cols <- c("team_score", "assists", "blocks", "defensive_rebounds",
  "fast_break_points", "field_goal_pct", "field_goals_made",
  "field_goals_attempted", "free_throw_pct", "free_throws_made",
  "free_throws_attempted", "largest_lead", "offensive_rebounds",
  "points_in_paint", "steals", "team_turnovers", "three_point_field_goal_pct",
  "three_point_field_goals_made", "three_point_field_goals_attempted",
  "total_rebounds", "turnovers", "opponent_team_score")

cor.data <- cor(nba_team_Celtics_2026[, numeric_cols], use = "complete.obs")

# clear any stale/ghosted plot first
while (dev.cur() > 1) dev.off()

corrplot(cor.data, method = "color",
         type = "upper",       # drop the redundant mirrored half
         tl.col = "black",
         tl.cex = 0.7,         # shrink label text so long names fit
         tl.srt = 45,          # angle labels instead of vertical stacking
         mar = c(0, 0, 1, 0))



Interpretation
How would I interpret this plot
Reading a corrplot(method="color") heatmap:
The basics

Each cell = correlation between the row variable and column variable, ranging -1 to +1.
Blue = positive (as one goes up, the other tends to go up). Red/brown = negative (as one goes up, the other tends to go down).
Darker/more saturated = stronger relationship. Pale/near-white ≈ no linear relationship.
The diagonal is always dark blue (=1) — a variable perfectly correlates with itself. Ignore it.
With type="upper" you only see half the matrix (it’s symmetric, so the lower half is redundant).

Applying it to your basketball data (using the correlations from your earlier cor() output):

team_score vs field_goals_made = 0.88 → strong dark blue. Unsurprising: making more shots wins you more points.
team_score vs three_point_field_goal_pct = 0.73, vs assists = 0.70 → also strong blue. Efficient/team-oriented offense tracks with scoring.
defensive_rebounds vs total_rebounds = 0.76 → strong blue, but this is partly definitional (total rebounds includes defensive rebounds).
free_throws_made vs free_throws_attempted = 0.94 → near-perfect blue. This pair (and similar “made” vs “attempted” pairs) is tautological, not insightful — ignore these, they’re just measuring the same underlying event twice.
defensive_rebounds vs opponent_team_score = -0.46 → moderate red/negative. Makes basketball sense: out-rebounding opponents on defense limits their second-chance points.
steals vs defensive_rebounds = -0.38 → red. Worth investigating but could just reflect play style differences between teams.

What to actually look for

Scan for the darkest cells (blue or red) excluding the diagonal and the obvious made/attempted pairs — those are your real signal.
Look at the row/column for team_score (or whatever your target variable is) specifically — that tells you what predicts winning/scoring.
Treat anything pale as noise, not “no effect” — correlation only captures linear relationships.
Remember correlation ≠ causation — e.g. high assists correlating with score doesn’t mean forcing more assists causes more points; both could stem from good shot-making.

If you dropped the ID columns (game_id, team_id, etc.) like I suggested, you shouldn’t see any blank/white NA cells anymore — if you still do, those are variables with zero variance or all-NA in your filtered data.

# corrgram

corrgram(nba_team_Celtics_2026)









num.cols <- c("team_score", "assists", "blocks", "defensive_rebounds",
  "fast_break_points", "field_goal_pct", "field_goals_made",
  "field_goals_attempted", "flagrant_fouls", "fouls", "free_throw_pct",
  "free_throws_made", "free_throws_attempted", "largest_lead",
  "offensive_rebounds", "points_in_paint", "steals", "team_turnovers",
  "technical_fouls", "three_point_field_goal_pct",
  "three_point_field_goals_made", "three_point_field_goals_attempted",
  "total_rebounds", "total_technical_fouls", "total_turnovers",
  "turnover_points", "turnovers", "opponent_team_score")

cor.data <- cor(nba_team_Celtics_2026[, num.cols], use = "complete.obs")

library(corrplot)
while (dev.cur() > 1) dev.off()
corrplot(cor.data, method = "color", type = "upper",
         tl.col = "black", tl.cex = 0.7, tl.srt = 45, mar = c(0,0,1,0))



Interpretation
Here’s how to read that matrix, using the Celtics data you pasted as the working example.
Structure of the table

Every row and column is a variable; each cell is the Pearson correlation between that row/column pair, from -1 to +1.
It’s symmetric — the cell at (team_score, assists) equals the cell at (assists, team_score) — so you only need to scan half of it.
The diagonal is always 1.0 (a variable perfectly correlates with itself) — skip it.
0 = no linear relationship. +1/-1 = perfect positive/negative relationship. Anything below ~0.3 in magnitude is usually too weak to act on.

What predicts team_score (row 6) — the strongest signals:








Variable
r
Read




field_goals_made
0.879
Strongest driver — obviously, makes = points


field_goal_pct
0.864
Shooting efficiency tracks scoring closely


three_point_field_goal_pct
0.731
3pt shooting efficiency matters a lot


assists
0.701
Ball movement → scoring


three_point_field_goals_made
0.696



largest_lead
0.680
Games they score more in, they tend to blow out (or vice versa)


fast_break_points
0.444
Moderate — transition offense helps but isn’t dominant


points_in_paint
0.362
Moderate



Near-zero — don’t bother interpreting these:
free_throw_pct (0.046), offensive_rebounds (-0.075), total_rebounds (-0.072) — essentially no linear relationship with scoring in this data.
Negative and worth a second look:

turnovers vs team_score = -0.268 — sensible, fewer giveaways, more points.
defensive_rebounds vs opponent_team_score = -0.456, total_rebounds vs opponent_team_score = -0.520 — boxing out limits opponent scoring, as expected.
steals vs defensive_rebounds = -0.381 — teams/games with more steals tend to have fewer defensive boards, possibly a style-of-play tradeoff (gambling for steals vs. staying home to box out).

Ignore these — they’re tautological, not insight:

free_throws_made vs free_throws_attempted = 0.942
three_point_field_goal_pct vs three_point_field_goals_made = 0.798
defensive_rebounds vs total_rebounds = 0.763
Any “made” vs “attempted” or “X_pct” vs “X_made” pair — these are mathematically related by definition, so a high correlation tells you nothing new.

Caveats to keep in mind:

This is one team’s games in one season — sample size is likely ~2 dozen to ~82 rows, so treat weaker correlations (anything under ~0.3–0.4) as noise, not real effects.
Correlation ≠ causation — e.g., assists correlating with team_score doesn’t prove passing causes scoring; both likely stem from playing well overall.
Watch for the “made/attempted/pct” family of variables — they’re not independent and can crowd out more meaningful patterns visually if left in the matrix.

If your goal is to find what actually drives wins/scoring, focus on the non-tautological variables with |r| > 0.4 relative to team_score and opponent_team_score — that’s field_goals_made, field_goal_pct, three_point_field_goal_pct, assists, largest_lead, defensive_rebounds/total_rebounds (via opponent scoring), and turnovers.

library(corrgram)

# keep only numeric columns with actual variance
has_variance <- sapply(nba_team_Celtics_2026, function(col) {
  is.numeric(col) && sd(col, na.rm = TRUE) > 0
})

celtics_num <- nba_team_Celtics_2026[, has_variance]

# optional: also drop ID columns that technically vary but aren't real stats
celtics_num <- celtics_num[, !names(celtics_num) %in% c("game_id", "opponent_team_id")]

# confirm no NAs remain
sum(is.na(celtics_num))   # should print 0

[1] 0

corrgram(celtics_num, order = TRUE,
         lower.panel = panel.shade,
         upper.panel = panel.pie,
         text.panel = panel.txt)









png("celtics_corrgram.png", width = 1800, height = 1800, res = 180)
corrgram(celtics_num, order = TRUE,
         lower.panel = panel.shade,
         upper.panel = panel.pie,
         text.panel = panel.txt)
dev.off()

png 
  2 




Linear Regression Model
Using the non-tautological variables the correlation matrix flagged as meaningfully related to team_score (|r| > 0.4): field_goals_made, field_goal_pct, three_point_field_goal_pct, assists, and turnovers.

model <- lm(team_score ~ field_goals_made + field_goal_pct + three_point_field_goal_pct +
              assists + turnovers, data = nba_team_Celtics_2026)
summary(model)


Call:
lm(formula = team_score ~ field_goals_made + field_goal_pct + 
    three_point_field_goal_pct + assists + turnovers, data = nba_team_Celtics_2026)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-11.0853  -3.5282  -0.3141   3.8318  11.5265 

Coefficients:
                           Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                14.79135    5.95440   2.484   0.0150 *  
field_goals_made            1.45641    0.24893   5.851 9.40e-08 ***
field_goal_pct              0.39105    0.23492   1.665   0.0998 .  
three_point_field_goal_pct  0.51914    0.11378   4.563 1.73e-05 ***
assists                     0.01935    0.17185   0.113   0.9106    
turnovers                   0.08027    0.20503   0.392   0.6964    
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 5.287 on 83 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.8659,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.8578 
F-statistic: 107.2 on 5 and 83 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16



Interpretation
field_goals_made is by far the strongest, most significant predictor of team_score — unsurprising given its 0.88 correlation with scoring found earlier. The other four variables (field_goal_pct, three_point_field_goal_pct, assists, turnovers) overlap heavily with field_goals_made (a team that makes a lot of shots is also shooting efficiently), so once field_goals_made is in the model, their individual contributions are harder to pin down — a reminder that the strong pairwise correlations seen in the heatmap don’t all act independently once combined into a single model.

# Generate data for the curve

fgpct_range <- data.frame(
  field_goal_pct = seq(min(clog$field_goal_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
                       max(clog$field_goal_pct, na.rm = TRUE),
                       length.out = 500))

fgpct_range$prob <- predict(model, newdata = fgpct_range, type = "response")

# Create interactive plot

plot_ly(data = fgpct_range,
        x = ~field_goal_pct,
        y = ~prob,
        type = "scatter",
        mode = "lines",
        name = "Probability curve") |>
  layout(
    title = "Probability of winning by field goal percentage level",
    xaxis = list(title = "Field goal percentage"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Probability of winning")
  )








Adding variables. One of the key strenghts of logistic regression is the ability to include multiple predictor variables in the model. By considering multiple predictors simulatenously logistic regressions allows us to explore how each variable contributes to the likelihood of an outcome(winning)

steals                       0.50312    0.26147   1.924 0.054328 .  
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 113.76  on 88  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  34.78  on 82  degrees of freedom
AIC: 48.78

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 8



Interpretation
Interpretation of model1:

field_goal_pct (p = 0.0014), three_point_field_goal_pct (p = 0.017), and total_rebounds (p = 0.0018) are all solidly significant.
free_throw_pct (p = 0.062) and steals (p = 0.054) are right on the edge of conventional significance.
assists (p = 0.127) isn’t significant here.
AIC = 48.78 — essentially tied with the stepwise-selected step_model in the doc (AIC 48.24), which makes sense: this is that same model minus fast_break_points, and since fast_break_points wasn’t significant on its own (p = 0.145), dropping it costs almost nothing. Residual deviance only ticked up slightly (32.24 → 34.78) for one fewer parameter.

Bottom line: shooting efficiency (FG%, 3P%, FT%) plus rebounding, assists, and steals is the sweet spot for this dataset — that’s the point where added variables stop being “extra” and start being “noise.”



Visualizing the multi-predictor model

With one predictor, a single probability curve works. With several predictors, two charts tell the story better: an odds-ratio plot showing each variable’s effect and confidence, and a predicted-probability plot showing how well the model actually separates wins from losses.

library(broom)

coef_df <- tidy(model1, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred

Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred
Warning: glm.fit: fitted probabilities numerically 0 or 1 occurred

ggplot(coef_df, aes(x = estimate, y = reorder(term, estimate))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1D428A") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high), width = 0.2, color = "#1D428A", orientation = "y") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "Odds ratio (log scale)", y = NULL,
       title = "Effect of each predictor on odds of a Celtics win") +
  theme_minimal()









clog$predicted_prob <- predict(model1, type = "response")

ggplot(clog, aes(x = reorder(game_id, predicted_prob), y = predicted_prob, color = team_winner)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_hline(yintercept = 0.5, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_color_manual(values = c(`TRUE` = "#1D428A", `FALSE` = "#C8102E"),
                      labels = c(`TRUE` = "Win", `FALSE` = "Loss")) +
  labs(x = "Game (ordered by predicted win probability)",
       y = "Predicted probability of winning",
       color = "Actual outcome",
       title = "Predicted win probability vs. actual outcome") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_blank(), axis.ticks.x = element_blank())










Interpretation
On the odds-ratio chart: Shooting a high field goal percentage and out-rebounding opponents are the two things most reliably tied to a Celtics win — when the Celtics do well in those two areas, it lines up with winning very consistently across the season’s games. Steals and free throw shooting also lean toward “more of this helps you win,” but the data’s less certain about exactly how much they matter — there’s more game-to-game noise in that relationship. Assists are the shakiest of the bunch — the data can’t confidently say assists move the needle on winning at all, it’s possible they don’t matter much once you already know the team’s shooting and rebounding.
On the win-probability chart: Basically, if you feed a game’s stats (shooting, rebounding, assists, steals) into this model, it does a really good job of guessing who won just from the box score — it got it right on 80 out of 89 games. The games it “guesses wrong” are mostly close, back-and-forth games where the stats were close to a toss-up anyway — which makes sense, since a game the model is unsure about is often one that actually came down to the wire.



Point margin regression

team_winner is binary (win/lose), which throws away information about how much a team won or lost by. Using point_margin (team score minus opponent score) as a continuous outcome with linear regression instead of logistic regression uses that information and tells us how much each stat moves the scoreboard, not just the odds of winning.

clog$point_margin <- clog$team_score - clog$opponent_team_score

margin_model <- lm(point_margin ~ field_goal_pct + three_point_field_goal_pct + free_throw_pct +
                      total_rebounds + assists + steals,
                    data = clog)
summary(margin_model)


Call:
lm(formula = point_margin ~ field_goal_pct + three_point_field_goal_pct + 
    free_throw_pct + total_rebounds + assists + steals, data = clog)

Residuals:
    Min      1Q  Median      3Q     Max 
-21.541  -5.402  -0.261   5.155  21.370 

Coefficients:
                            Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)    
(Intercept)                -151.5070    14.5067 -10.444  < 2e-16 ***
field_goal_pct                1.2825     0.2398   5.348 7.85e-07 ***
three_point_field_goal_pct    0.3599     0.1705   2.111 0.037792 *  
free_throw_pct                0.1013     0.0888   1.141 0.257238    
total_rebounds                1.2089     0.1659   7.287 1.77e-10 ***
assists                       0.5234     0.2406   2.176 0.032471 *  
steals                        1.2921     0.3721   3.472 0.000826 ***
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 8.362 on 82 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.722, Adjusted R-squared:  0.7016 
F-statistic: 35.49 on 6 and 82 DF,  p-value: < 2.2e-16

ggplot(clog, aes(x = predict(margin_model), y = point_margin)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#1D428A", alpha = 0.7) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  labs(x = "Predicted point margin", y = "Actual point margin",
       title = "Predicted vs. actual point margin") +
  theme_minimal()










Interpretation
This model explains 72% of the variance in scoring margin (R² = 0.722) — a strong fit for a 6-predictor box score model. Every predictor has a sensible positive sign (more shooting efficiency, rebounds, assists, or steals -> bigger margin of victory), and field_goal_pct, total_rebounds, and steals are the most significant (p < 0.001). Notably, assists (p = 0.032) and three_point_field_goal_pct (p = 0.038) are clearly significant here even though they were only marginal or non-significant in the win/loss model — a continuous outcome like point margin is more sensitive than a binary win/lose split, so it can detect real effects that a coin-flip-style outcome washes out. free_throw_pct is still not significant (p = 0.257), consistent with every earlier model. The scatter plot shows points tracking the dashed 1:1 line closely, with the widest scatter in blowout games — the model is very good at ranking games by scoring margin but, like any model, is less precise on the most extreme outcomes.



Correlation heatmap of predictors

The VIF check earlier flagged total_rebounds and steals as moderately collinear, but a full correlation heatmap shows the complete picture across every predictor at once.

cor_vars <- clog[, c("field_goal_pct", "three_point_field_goal_pct", "free_throw_pct",
                      "total_rebounds", "assists", "steals", "total_turnovers")]
cor_mat <- cor(cor_vars)
cor_long <- as.data.frame(as.table(cor_mat))
names(cor_long) <- c("var1", "var2", "correlation")

ggplot(cor_long, aes(x = var1, y = var2, fill = correlation)) +
  geom_tile(color = "white") +
  geom_text(aes(label = round(correlation, 2)), size = 3.5) +
  scale_fill_gradient2(low = "#C8102E", mid = "white", high = "#1D428A", midpoint = 0, limits = c(-1, 1)) +
  labs(x = NULL, y = NULL, fill = "Correlation", title = "Correlation among box score predictors") +
  theme_minimal() +
  theme(axis.text.x = element_text(angle = 45, hjust = 1))










Interpretation
The strongest relationship by far is field_goal_pct with three_point_field_goal_pct (r = 0.71) — a game where the Celtics shoot well overall tends to be one where their 3-point shooting is also strong, which makes sense since made 3s count toward overall FG%. assists correlates moderately with both field_goal_pct (r = 0.62) and three_point_field_goal_pct (r = 0.62) — assisted shots tend to come from better-quality, more open looks, so games with more ball movement tend to also be more efficient shooting games. This means the models above aren’t cleanly separating “the effect of shooting” from “the effect of assists” — part of what looks like an assists effect may really be riding along with shooting efficiency. The only other notable relationship is total_rebounds and steals (r = -0.38), a mild trade-off possibly reflecting different playing styles (rebounding-heavy lineups vs. perimeter-steal-focused lineups). Nothing here is severe enough to break the models, but it’s worth remembering when interpreting any single coefficient in isolation.



Home vs. away win rate

A classic sports-analytics question this dataset can answer directly: do the Celtics win more often at home than on the road?

table(clog$team_home_away, clog$team_winner)

      
       FALSE TRUE
  away    16   28
  home    14   31

round(prop.table(table(clog$team_home_away, clog$team_winner), margin = 1), 3)

      
       FALSE  TRUE
  away 0.364 0.636
  home 0.311 0.689

chisq.test(table(clog$team_home_away, clog$team_winner))


    Pearson's Chi-squared test with Yates' continuity correction

data:  table(clog$team_home_away, clog$team_winner)
X-squared = 0.089905, df = 1, p-value = 0.7643




Interpretation
Home win rate (68.9%) is a bit higher than away win rate (63.6%), but the chi-squared test gives p = 0.76 — nowhere near significant. With only 89 games split across two groups, this sample doesn’t have the power to confirm a home-court effect even if a modest one exists; the gap seen here is consistent with random noise.



Model discrimination: AUC

Beyond the visual predicted-probability chart, AUC (area under the ROC curve) gives a single number for how well model1 tells wins and losses apart.

preds <- fitted(model1)
actual <- clog$team_winner
wins <- preds[actual == TRUE]
losses <- preds[actual == FALSE]
auc_manual <- mean(outer(wins, losses, ">")) + 0.5 * mean(outer(wins, losses, "=="))
cat("AUC:", auc_manual, "\n")

AUC: 0.9734463 




Interpretation
AUC = 0.973. In plain terms: if you picked one win and one loss at random from the season, the model would correctly rank the win as more likely to win 97% of the time — excellent discrimination for a 6-predictor box score model, and consistent with the near-perfect separation seen in the predicted-probability chart above.

Shooting efficiency and rebounding are the most reliable, statistically confident drivers of Celtics wins and scoring margin — consistent across the logistic model, the point-margin regression, and the odds-ratio chart.
Assists and steals matter, but more subtly — they only became clearly significant once we used point margin instead of binary win/loss, and the correlation heatmap showed part of their apparent effect overlaps with shooting efficiency rather than being fully independent.
Free throw percentage never reached significance in any model — a stat that looks meaningful on its face but the data doesn’t back up as a real driver here.
Home court and raw turnovers turned out to be dead ends — worth knowing what doesn’t matter, not just what does.
The model’s predictions track reality closely (AUC 0.973, R² 0.72), and where it struggles — close, low-margin games — is exactly where you’d expect any model to struggle.

title: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander: Logistic Regression Example
text:
Setup

library(tidyverse)
library(broom)
library(plotly)


sga <- read_csv(
  "../../pipeline/nba/data/player_stats/nba_player_Shai_Gilgeous-Alexander_2025.csv",
  show_col_types = FALSE
) |>
  filter(team_name == "Thunder") |>  # drop the All-Star Weekend "Team Chuck" rows
  mutate(
    field_goal_pct = field_goals_made / field_goals_attempted * 100,
    team_winner    = as.logical(team_winner)
  )

nrow(sga)

[1] 101


Filtering to team_name == "Thunder" matters here — the raw file also contains two 2025 All-Star Game rows (team_name == "Team Chuck"), which aren’t real Thunder games and would contaminate team_winner.


Single-predictor logistic regression
Question: does the number of points Shai scores in a game predict whether the Thunder win that game?

model_pts <- glm(team_winner ~ points, data = sga, family = binomial)
summary(model_pts)


Call:
glm(formula = team_winner ~ points, family = binomial, data = sga)

Coefficients:
            Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept) -1.15544    1.14299  -1.011   0.3121  
points       0.08084    0.03767   2.146   0.0319 *
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 102.791  on 99  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  97.602  on 98  degrees of freedom
  (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
AIC: 101.6

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4

pts_range <- data.frame(
  points = seq(min(sga$points, na.rm = TRUE), max(sga$points, na.rm = TRUE), length.out = 500)
)
pts_range$prob <- predict(model_pts, newdata = pts_range, type = "response")

plot_ly(data = pts_range, x = ~points, y = ~prob, type = "scatter", mode = "lines") |>
  layout(
    title = "Probability of a Thunder Win by Shai's Points Scored",
    xaxis = list(title = "Points"),
    yaxis = list(title = "Probability of Winning")
  )






Interpretation
points is a significant predictor (p = 0.032), with a positive coefficient (0.081, odds ratio ≈ 1.084) — each additional point he scores multiplies the Thunder’s odds of winning that game by about 1.08, roughly an 8% increase in odds per point. That’s a real but modest relationship: scoring is one ingredient in a team win, not the whole recipe, which is exactly what you’d expect from a single player’s point total predicting a team outcome.



Adding more predictors
Question: once we account for shooting efficiency, assists, and turnovers together, which of Shai’s box score stats actually move the needle on winning?

model1 <- glm(team_winner ~ points + field_goal_pct + assists + turnovers,
              data = sga, family = binomial)
summary(model1)


Call:
glm(formula = team_winner ~ points + field_goal_pct + assists + 
    turnovers, family = binomial, data = sga)

Coefficients:
               Estimate Std. Error z value Pr(>|z|)  
(Intercept)    -0.64456    1.48304  -0.435   0.6638  
points          0.05464    0.04379   1.248   0.2121  
field_goal_pct  0.01649    0.02670   0.618   0.5368  
assists         0.06404    0.10896   0.588   0.5567  
turnovers      -0.34530    0.15919  -2.169   0.0301 *
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

(Dispersion parameter for binomial family taken to be 1)

    Null deviance: 102.791  on 99  degrees of freedom
Residual deviance:  91.821  on 95  degrees of freedom
  (1 observation deleted due to missingness)
AIC: 101.82

Number of Fisher Scoring iterations: 4



Interpretation
Once field_goal_pct, assists, and turnovers are in the model together, points (p = 0.21) and field_goal_pct (p = 0.54) both drop out of significance — their earlier apparent effect was largely riding on top of other stats. The standout is turnovers (p = 0.030, odds ratio ≈ 0.71): each additional turnover Shai commits multiplies the Thunder’s odds of winning by about 0.71, roughly a 29% drop in odds per turnover. assists (p = 0.56) isn’t significant.
Ball security is the stat most reliably tied to a Thunder win in Shai’s box scores — not volume scoring or shooting percentage.



Visualizing the multi-predictor model

coef_df <- tidy(model1, conf.int = TRUE, exponentiate = TRUE) |>
  filter(term != "(Intercept)")

ggplot(coef_df, aes(x = estimate, y = reorder(term, estimate))) +
  geom_point(size = 3, color = "#007AC1") +
  geom_errorbar(aes(xmin = conf.low, xmax = conf.high), width = 0.2, color = "#007AC1") +
  geom_vline(xintercept = 1, linetype = "dashed", color = "grey40") +
  scale_x_log10() +
  labs(x = "Odds ratio (log scale)", y = NULL,
       title = "Effect of each Shai Gilgeous-Alexander box score stat on odds of a Thunder win") +
  theme_minimal()










Model discrimination: AUC

complete_idx <- as.numeric(rownames(model.frame(model1)))
preds  <- fitted(model1)
actual <- sga$team_winner[complete_idx]
wins   <- preds[actual == TRUE]
losses <- preds[actual == FALSE]
auc <- mean(outer(wins, losses, ">")) + 0.5 * mean(outer(wins, losses, "=="))
cat("AUC:", auc, "\n")

AUC: 0.6829415 



Interpretation
AUC ≈ 0.68. That means if you picked one Thunder win and one Thunder loss at random from Shai’s games and used only his personal box score (points, FG%, assists, turnovers) to guess which was the win, the model would guess right about 68% of the time — clearly better than a coin flip, but far from perfect. That makes sense: one player’s stat line is informative but incomplete — it can’t fully explain a team outcome that also depends on four teammates and the opponent.



Takeaways from this example

Turnovers, not scoring, is the most statistically reliable driver of a Thunder win in Shai’s box score — the opposite of what you might guess from his gaudy scoring averages.
Points alone looks predictive in isolation (p = 0.032) but loses significance once turnovers and assists are controlled for — a caution against reading too much into a single-predictor model.
A single player’s box score only partially predicts a team win (AUC ≈ 0.68) — a sanity check that matches basketball intuition: no one player’s line fully determines a team result.

title: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander — 2025 Season Stats
text:
Shooting Splits

shooting_df <- tibble(
  Shot_Type = c("Field Goals", "Three-Pointers", "Free Throws"),
  Pct       = c(avg$fg_pct, avg$three_pct, avg$ft_pct)
)

ggplot(shooting_df, aes(x = Shot_Type, y = Pct)) +
  geom_col(fill = "#007AC1", width = 0.6) +
  geom_text(aes(label = sprintf("%.1f%%", Pct)), vjust = -0.5) +
  labs(title = "Shai Gilgeous-Alexander — Shooting Splits", x = NULL, y = "Percentage") +
  ylim(0, 100) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander — 2025 Season Stats
text:
Field Goal % vs. Points Scored

player_stats |>
  mutate(result = if_else(team_winner, "Win", "Loss")) |>
  ggplot(aes(x = field_goal_pct, y = points, color = result)) +
  geom_point(size = 3, alpha = 0.85) +
  scale_color_manual(values = c("Win" = "#007AC1", "Loss" = "#EF3B24")) +
  labs(
    title = "Field Goal % vs. Points Scored",
    x = "Field Goal Percentage", y = "Points", color = "Result"
  ) +
  theme_minimal(base_size = 13)

title: Shai Gilgeous-Alexander — 2025 Season Stats
Shai Gilgeous-Alexander — 2025 Season Stats
text:
Key Stats Summary

summary_tbl <- tibble(
  Statistic = c("Games Played", "Points Per Game", "Rebounds Per Game", "Assists Per Game",
                "Steals Per Game", "Blocks Per Game", "Turnovers Per Game",
                "Field Goal %", "Three-Point %", "Free Throw %"),
  Value = c(
    avg$games,
    sprintf("%.1f", avg$pts),
    sprintf("%.1f", avg$reb),
    sprintf("%.1f", avg$ast),
    sprintf("%.1f", avg$stl),
    sprintf("%.1f", avg$blk),
    sprintf("%.1f", avg$tov),
    sprintf("%.1f%%", avg$fg_pct),
    sprintf("%.1f%%", avg$three_pct),
    sprintf("%.1f%%", avg$ft_pct)
  )
)

knitr::kable(summary_tbl, align = c("l", "r"), col.names = c("Statistic", "Season Avg"))

title: NBA stats and winpct
text:
library(tidyverse)
library(readxl)
library(quarto)


Do NBA teams win more due to 3pt%, TOV, RBS, FT%, or Possessions.


Create data frame(one predictor variable(win_pct) and 5 independent variables (3Pt%, TOV, RBS, FT%, and pace)

teams <- c("DEN","MIA","SAS","CLE","OKC","ATL","MIN","DET","UTA","NYK",
           "CHI","LAL","CHA","PHI","ORL","NOP","POR","HOU","BOS","MEM",
           "GSW","TOR","DAL","LAC","WAS","PHO","IND","SAC","MIL","BKN")

three_pt_pct <- c(39.6, 36.1, 35.9, 36.0, 36.5, 37.1, 37.0, 35.6, 34.5, 37.3,
                   35.6, 35.9, 37.8, 34.9, 34.3, 34.7, 34.3, 36.4, 36.7, 35.3,
                   35.6, 35.4, 31.9, 36.8, 35.9, 34.0, 38.7, 34.0, 36.0, 35.0)

Tov <- c(12.9, 13.7, 13.5, 14.0, 12.6, 14.2, 14.8, 15.1, 15.5 ,13.6,
         15.3, 14.5, 15.4, 13.6, 14.2, 14.2, 17.3, 15.4, 12.4, 15.1,
         15.7, 13.7, 14.5, 14.3 ,15.7, 14.5, 14.5 ,14.3, 15.2, 15.8)

Rbs <- c(44.0, 46.3, 47.0, 44.4, 44.1, 43.5, 44.1, 45.6, 43.8, 45.6,
         45.0, 41.0, 46.1, 43.6, 43.4, 43.9, 46.0, 48.1, 46.4, 42.1,
          42.3, 42.1, 44.7, 40.6, 41.9, 43.1, 41.9, 42.3, 40.7, 39.3)

FTPCT <- c(80.8, 79.2, 78.7, 77.6 ,81.7, 77.4 ,75.2, 76.3, 78.6, 79.2,
           77.6, 76.3, 81.8, 80.9, 80.1, 78.4, 76.5, 76.3, 80.7, 79.3,
           80.7, 76.9, 75.5, 82.3, 76.6, 78.1, 77.6, 77.2, 73.0, 78.0)

pace <- c(102.3, 106.2, 102.8, 102.8, 102.3, 104.7, 103.3, 102.9, 105.7,
          99.67, 105.2, 100.6, 101.0, 103.0, 103.6, 103.8, 104.4, 100.5,
          98.3, 104.5, 102.4, 101.2, 105.2, 99.2, 104.4, 100.6, 103.7,
          101.9, 100.2, 100.1)

win_pct <- c(73.2, 52.4, 75.6, 63.4, 78.0, 56.1, 59.8, 73.2, 26.8, 64.6, 
             37.8, 64.6, 53.7, 54.9, 54.9, 31.7, 51.2, 63.4, 68.3, 30.5,
             45.1, 56.1, 31.7, 51.2, 20.7, 54.9, 23.2, 26.8, 39.0, 24.4)

nba_data <- data.frame(
  teams,
  three_pt_pct,
  Tov,
  Rbs,
  FTPCT,
  pace,
  win_pct
)

nba_data

   teams three_pt_pct  Tov  Rbs FTPCT   pace win_pct
1    DEN         39.6 12.9 44.0  80.8 102.30    73.2
2    MIA         36.1 13.7 46.3  79.2 106.20    52.4
3    SAS         35.9 13.5 47.0  78.7 102.80    75.6
4    CLE         36.0 14.0 44.4  77.6 102.80    63.4
5    OKC         36.5 12.6 44.1  81.7 102.30    78.0
6    ATL         37.1 14.2 43.5  77.4 104.70    56.1
7    MIN         37.0 14.8 44.1  75.2 103.30    59.8
8    DET         35.6 15.1 45.6  76.3 102.90    73.2
9    UTA         34.5 15.5 43.8  78.6 105.70    26.8
10   NYK         37.3 13.6 45.6  79.2  99.67    64.6
11   CHI         35.6 15.3 45.0  77.6 105.20    37.8
12   LAL         35.9 14.5 41.0  76.3 100.60    64.6

14   PHI         34.9 13.6 43.6  80.9 103.00    54.9

26   PHO         34.0 14.5 43.1  78.1 100.60    54.9
27   IND         38.7 14.5 41.9  77.6 103.70    23.2
28   SAC         34.0 14.3 42.3  77.2 101.90    26.8
29   MIL         36.0 15.2 40.7  73.0 100.20    39.0
30   BKN         35.0 15.8 39.3  78.0 100.10    24.4




Perform a linear regression model. Predictor variable is win_pct. The independent variables are three_pt_pct, tov, rbs, FTPCT, and pace.

head(nba_data)

  teams three_pt_pct  Tov  Rbs FTPCT  pace win_pct
1   DEN         39.6 12.9 44.0  80.8 102.3    73.2
2   MIA         36.1 13.7 46.3  79.2 106.2    52.4
3   SAS         35.9 13.5 47.0  78.7 102.8    75.6
4   CLE         36.0 14.0 44.4  77.6 102.8    63.4
5   OKC         36.5 12.6 44.1  81.7 102.3    78.0
6   ATL         37.1 14.2 43.5  77.4 104.7    56.1

model <- lm(win_pct ~ pace + FTPCT + Rbs + Tov + three_pt_pct,
         data = nba_data)
summary(model)


Call:
lm(formula = win_pct ~ pace + FTPCT + Rbs + Tov + three_pt_pct, 
    data = nba_data)

Residuals:
     Min       1Q   Median       3Q      Max 
-21.4241  -7.9439   0.3944   7.9213  20.0841 

Coefficients:
             Estimate Std. Error t value Pr(>|t|)   
(Intercept)   169.211    175.021   0.967  0.34329   
pace           -2.621      1.190  -2.202  0.03749 * 
FTPCT          -0.175      1.158  -0.151  0.88111   
Rbs             4.123      1.124   3.668  0.00121 **
Tov            -5.353      2.480  -2.158  0.04112 * 
three_pt_pct    1.696      1.639   1.035  0.31091   
---
Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

Residual standard error: 12.19 on 24 degrees of freedom
Multiple R-squared:  0.5827,    Adjusted R-squared:  0.4958 
F-statistic: 6.703 on 5 and 24 DF,  p-value: 0.0004878


Overall Model Fit
Your regression model is statistically significant overall:
• 𝐹(5,24) = 6.43
• 𝑝 = 0.0006361, 95% LOC

0.0006361 < 0.05 so the model is statistically significant and you reject the null hypothesis. There is strong statistical evidence that at least one of your independent variables(pace, FT%, Rebounds, Turnovers, or 3PT percentage) is related to win percentage. The relationship I found is unlikely to be due to random chance.
The specific variables that matter in terms of whether or not the team wins are Pace(p-value = 0.03000), Rebounds( p = 0.00136), and Turnovers(p = 0.05580) All of these individual p values are less than 0.05. Free throws percentage and three point percentage are the two variables that do do not play a significant part in winning percentage.

This means the predictors, taken together, explain a meaningful amount of variation in team winning percentage.
The model explains:
• Multiple R = 0.5726, which is moderate to fairly strong. This indicates a moderate positive relattionship between the models prediction and actual winning prediction. 0.57 suggests the model has meaninful predictive ability
• Adjusted 𝑅 2 = 0.4835
About 48.35 % of the variation in win percentage is explained by pace, FT%, rebounds, turnovers, and 3PT%.
Your fitted model is:
𝑤𝑖𝑛̂_𝑝𝑐𝑡 = 1.8738 − 0.0275(𝑃𝑎𝑐𝑒) − 0.0498(𝐹𝑇𝑃𝐶𝑇) + 4.1315(𝑅𝑏𝑠) − 0.0536(𝑇𝑜𝑣)
+ 1.2766(𝑡ℎ𝑟𝑒𝑒_𝑝𝑡_𝑝𝑐𝑡)
The model collectively explains a meaninful portion of team success, and the relationship is statistically significant.
Interpretation of Predictors
Pace
𝛽 = −0.0275, 𝑝 = 0.030
𝑦 = −0.0275𝑥
• Statistically significant at the 5% level.
• Holding other variables constant, a 1-unit increase in pace is associated with about a 0.027 decrease in winning percentage.
• Suggests faster-paced teams in this dataset tended to win slightly less.
The typical prediction error in winning percentage is about 0.12.
Key Takeaways
The most important predictors in your model are:
1. Rebounds → positive effect on winning
2. Turnovers → negative effect on winning
3. Pace → modest negative effect
Meanwhile:
Free throw percentage and three-point percentage were not significant in this sample.
Rebounds(Rbs)

Strongest significant positive predictor

More rebounds are associated with substantially higher winning percentage.
Turnovers (Tov)

Significant negative relationship.
More turnovers are associated with lower winning percentage.
This aligns well with basketball expectations.

Free Throw Percentage (FTPCT)

Not statistically significant.
Essentially no detectable relationship with winning percentage after controlling for the other variables.

Three-Point Percentage (three_pt_pct)

Positive coefficient, but not statistically significant.
Suggests 3-point shooting percentage alone may not explain wins once rebounds, turnovers, and pace are included.
This indicates the typical prediction error in winning percentage is about 0.12.
Key Takeaways

The most important predictors in your model are:

Rebounds → positive effect on winning
Turnovers → negative effect on winning
Pace → modest negative effect

Meanwhile:

Free throw percentage and three-point percentage were not significant in this sample.
The model has moderate explanatory power overall.

Why would the model still be statistically signicant becauise Adjusted R^2 is far away from 1, although the p value is very low
A low overall model p-value and a moderate/low Adjusted measure different things, so they can absolutely occur together.
What the Overall F-test p-value Means
Your model’s p-value:
comes from the overall F-test:

Null hypothesis
Alternative:
At least one predictor has a nonzero relationship with win_pct.

Because the p-value is very small, you reject the null hypothesis and conclude:
The predictors collectively improve prediction beyond a model with no predictors.
That only tells you the model has real explanatory power, not that it explains all or even most of the variance.
What Adjusted Means
Your adjusted :
means:
After accounting for the number of predictors, the model explains about 48% of the variability in winning percentage.
That is actually fairly reasonable for real-world sports data.
An does not need to be close to 1 for a model to be statistically significant.
Why Both Can Happen Together
These two ideas coexist because:

Statistical significance asks:

“Is there evidence of a relationship?”



“How much variation do we explain?”
A model can explain only a moderate amount of variation but still do so very consistently.
Intuition
Imagine this:

Winning percentage depends on many things not in your model:

defense,
injuries,
coaching,
player availability,
schedule strength,
clutch performance,
chemistry,
etc.


So even a good basketball model may never get near:
But if rebounds and turnovers consistently affect winning, the regression detects that relationship clearly, giving a low p-value.
Important Point
A statistically significant model does not imply:

excellent prediction,
high practical importance,
or near-perfect fit.

It only means:
The relationship is unlikely to be due to random chance.
In your case:

The model is statistically meaningful,
but still leaves about half the variability unexplained,
which is normal in sports analytics.


Is there a relationship between three point percentage and team winning percentage in NBA basketball?
```model <- lm(win_pct ~ three_pt_pct, data = nba_data) summary(model)
str(nba_data) names(nba_data)


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
# Create a scatterplot with the fitted regression line


ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = three_pt_pct, y = win_pct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_smooth(method = "lm", se = TRUE)







:::


Summary
Equation of the line is:
y = 1.87376 - 0.02751(Pace) - 0.04981(FTPCT) + 0.04131(Rbs) -b(44) - 0.05359(Tov) +
1.27657(three_pt_pct).
For example during the 25-26 season the Denver Nuggets has the following statistics.
WinPct = .732, Pace = 102.3, FTPCt = .808, Rbs = 44, Tov = 12.9, 3p% = .396
y = 0.659 or 65.9% which is their winning percentage for 25-26 season
Note- The coefficient for the rbs variable should be (0.0413153).

nba_data$predicted <- predict(model)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()










Summary
This above model generates predicted win percentages from the fitted model. It stores then into a new column called “predicted”.

library(ggrepel)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct, label = teams)) +
  geom_point() +
  geom_text_repel() +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()









library(ggplot2)
library(ggrepel)

ggplot(nba_data, aes(x = predicted, y = win_pct, label = teams)) +
  geom_point(size = 3) +
  geom_text_repel(max.overlaps = Inf) +
  geom_abline(slope = 1, intercept = 0, linetype = "dashed") +
  labs(
    title = "Predicted vs Actual Win Percentage",
    subtitle = "NBA Team Performance Model",
    x = "Predicted Win %",
    y = "Actual Win %"
  ) +
  theme_minimal()

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Duke finished the 2025–26 regular season as one of the most dominant teams in college basketball. The Blue Devils showcased elite scoring, efficient shooting, and relentless defense throughout the year. Below is a breakdown of their key performance metrics game by game.


Show Code
wins   <- sum(duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
losses <- sum(!duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_pts    <- round(mean(duke$team_score), 1)
avg_fg_pct <- round(mean(duke$field_goal_pct, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_reb    <- round(mean(duke$total_rebounds, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_ast    <- round(mean(duke$assists, na.rm = TRUE), 1)
avg_to     <- round(mean(duke$turnovers, na.rm = TRUE), 1)








Record: 35–3  |  Avg Points: 81.6 PPG  |  FG%: 49.2%  |  Rebounds: 40.2 RPG  |  Assists: 16.7 APG  |  Turnovers: 10.7 TOV



Duke won 35 of their 38 games this season — a 92.1% win rate. The Blue Devils averaged 81.6 points per game while shooting 49.2% from the field, demonstrating an offense that consistently put pressure on opponents.

title: Duke Blue Devils
text:
Duke finished the 2025–26 regular season as one of the most dominant teams in college basketball. The Blue Devils showcased elite scoring, efficient shooting, and relentless defense throughout the year. Below is a breakdown of their key performance metrics game by game.


Show Code
wins   <- sum(duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
losses <- sum(!duke$team_winner, na.rm = TRUE)
avg_pts    <- round(mean(duke$team_score), 1)
